Inspect my current project to see whats already been completed. I use the local/ directory to store planning documents and memory supplements that are gitignored. This is a dockerized web app that was originally developed to automate setting a strava description when new runs were found, that transformed into a web ui gui description builder, a heatmap trend visualization page, a clone of my spreadsheet I use to plan my upcoming running schedule, a local api and web ui to issue those commands, and a web ui page to enter all the external api sources. I am actually very happy with where it is currently at but I have have a few features that I really would like to explore and brainstorm. 1. Right now profiles are hardcoded, I would like for maybe a yaml profile builder framework and web UI so that I can modify, or add, custom profiles (a profile is just an activity classifier so we can apply different description templates). 2. I have just begun to scratch the surface of workouts, I have implemented a Pace

Analyze the existing codebase and produce useful documentation.

I put all of the planning doc's (that are gitignored) in the local/ dir, those may be good for an overview of what is going on but otherwise you should be able to get all the info from the project files themselves.

No the next steps you provided: 1. Review docs/index.md to familiarize yourself with the documentation structure.
  2. For a brownfield PRD, point the PRD workflow to docs/index.md.
  3. For UI-only features: reference docs/architecture-android.md.
  4. For API-only features: reference docs/architecture-backend.md.
  5. For full-stack features: reference both architectures + docs/integration-architecture.md.

1. just the web app. 2. fitness/training, and personal analytics. 3. none, this is local self hosted FOSS. 4. The primary user is a hobby runner, who wants to plan out their week, have an automated strava description that collates all their favorite data points from multiple sources, and have a local place to view training trends that matter to them (customization). 5. under the hood is high complexity, with algorithms backed by medical science. To the user this shuld feel easy, quick, intuitive. If we do everything right, the user should spend a minimal amount of time using the app (as oppsed to strava/garmin business model), I love automations as well, the best button is one you never have to press because it knows when to press itself.

1. I can only speak for myself, but all of the current features have been developed because i took my current self-training approach and combined every aspect of what I was doing, what apps I was checking, what data I actually cared about, and what I wanted to document in my descriptions, and made it into this single tool. idk if other hobby runners would appriciate my opinionated vision but I sure do. I think to delight other users and get them wanting to install this is to start with just a whimsical strava description, like instead of saying this run was 1050 calories we can convert to seiverts of radiation in an equivalent amount of bananas (1.0 micrseiverts) thats just one of potentially hundredds of whimsical conversions across not only calories but time, distance, elevation, heartbeats, etc.  2. This is not suposed to be better than strava/garmin, it's suposed to compliment, to automatically add data to strava that normally isn't in strava, to easily configure and send a workout to garmin because using their UI and calendar is such a PIA, to quickly war-game differnt mileages throughout the week to see if its high risk or detraining or optimal. 3. I've used 6+ different tools, all with and without premium subscriptions from time to time, and this is just how I can get all of that benifit without any cost all in a single place. 4. I chose the name Chonicle because it relects a story arch, a journy, one that is viewed in a heatmap, told through automated descriptions, and quickly planned so you can spend more time running.

1. User outcomes: Use the automated description generator to be creative and fun, Use the planning to quickly war-game to lower injury risk, Use a workout builder to quickly and easily build workouts and send to garmin (create garmin workout and set on calendar so I just click 'run' on my watch and it asks if I wnt to do todays workout) (this feature not built yet. 2.aha moment would be using the jinja fields to customize and create any custom desc they want. 3. My non techy dad to use it, and having enough good features that my brother would be motivated to figure out how to install docker to run it. 4. zero manual interventions per week, a quick and snappy ui, confidence the custom descriptions display what you want in real world. 5. it already is valueable to me right now, some luxury/premium polish in the UI/UX would go a long way. 6. First: right now profiles are hardcoded, I would like for maybe a yaml profile builder framework and web UI so that I can modify, or add, custom profiles in the web ui (a profile is just an activity classifier so we can apply different description templates). Second: I have just begun to scratch the surface of workouts, I have implemented a Pace Workshop to find training paces, but I would like a Workout Workshop that would be like a yaml workout builder that I can easily add to a days workout. Third: The ability to send a day (or days) to garmin (create a garmin workout, create a garmin calendar workout for that day, make this process so easy and seamless to go from inputting running workout shorthand to having my watch ask if I want to do todays workout.  7. Vision: That the UX is so good I actually keep up with using this long term, both my dad and brother use this, and we can use it to share/compare/compete. That a community is developed around sharing custom profiles, templates, and workouts, that all can be easily shared and imported/exported, that there will be a creative corner in a forum/github where people can show off their custom descriptions.

1. They are starting with setting up sources, then modifying default templates and enabling different profiles, then inputing their upcoming running plan (next 2-4 weeks). Critical value moment is after testing that the description works by rerunning on latest activity. 2. Broken template, and lack of knowledge using Jinja formatting. 3. Dad's goal is to just have fun descriptions like calories to shots of Jameson. Generating a strava api app is stressful and hard, I have oauth baked in but we still need to generate that My API App in strava. Step-by-step walkthrough guide needed. Once its been set up, tested and he no longer has to touch it. 4. He works in tech all day, and doesn't want to go through the hassle at home. He'll keep it because its automated and he won't have to do anything and it'll keep working. 5. maintenance is just updating your next week and the followings training plan. API token failures, and breaking changes on updates. This is self hosted so proper documentation on github issues, alarms and alerts if things arn't healthy with a troubleshooting guide. 6. Create a yaml in the web ui for a workout, that workout is selectable in the dropdown (fuzzy filter searchable?) when Run Type = SOS, three dot menu 'Send to Garmin', this would create a workout if not already in garmin and set this workout for the date of the run. This repo did this and we can borrow as much or little inspiration from it https://github.com/yeekang-0311/garmin_planner

1. I pull in all activity data from 5 different sources' API and have that available in jinja to build a custom description. 2. No need to take time writing descriptions everyday. 3. A general moderately techy users ability to use jinja to create a template that works. 4. Use defaults. 5. FOSS, many tools available but this is a simple consolidation of what you need, not an Elite level tool to get you to the Olympics, but a quick hobby runner tool to help avoid injury and avoid waisting time looking up 5 different apps.

1. idk what those mean, this is just a selfhosted docker web app. 2. Chrome/ff/opera 3. Nah, I would want to eventually post to r/selfhosted and r/strava maybe r/runninglifestyle maybe have a blog that reviews it. 4. Not other than the heartbeat that checks for recent activities. 5. none for now.

It's currently MPA but that is only because I've added pages and features over time, idk what I'm doing and if the proper way to handle this is SPA then I'm all for it, but it's not broke now so idk if it needs fixin.  Confirm all others.
1. Experience MVP. 2. Already have most of those, but I would chose UI/UX polish, profile builder, workout workshop, garmin workout send. 3-7 I'll let you decide based on your recommendations

I just wanted to let you know that I am heavily invested in github with this project, new features and issues are tracked using the issues functionality, new implementaions are on their own branch and then when validated merged with main, propper git documentaion, lessons learned documented. I just want you to properly use github for any and all changes.

1. < 1s. 2. < 1 failed description write per month. 3. months on end, silently running in the background. 4. within 15 - 30 sec? doesn't have to be instant, whatever is reasonable. 5. sliding scale, whatever industry standard is for this situation. 6. proper way to handle api keys and tokens and credentials, if there is a need to access remotely this is meant to be used via tailscale and not exposed to the open internet, otherwise this is just a local web app.
1. I'm not sure what paths or documents would be benificial to you or what I would want to include if anything, this is my first run through of bmad method. 2. default is fine

Ok my PRD is in great shape and ready for downstream work, what are my next steps?
I'm happy with the prd, lets move on to 2. Create UX with sally

1. Walled gardens, all have usefull things but not all in the same place. 2. Something where they can spend a little bit of time upfront being creative configuring custom templates, then set and forget. Then they would only really need to interact every few weeks when updating their training plan. Low touch, low time sink, Strava descriptions become a productive snapshot and they can sped time socilaizing on other platforms or running, but the analytics and planning is easy, minimal, and taken care of. 3. The typical user will have to be able to install docker, run dockers, load an internal web service, familiar enough with tailscale to access remotely or to share. 4. For Viewing: any device, Planning: any device, for Creating templates/profiles/workouts: desktop/laptop, for initial config: desktop/laptop. 5. Setting up auto descriptions is a one time set and forget, the real major touch point is the sunday planning.

1. Update daily mileage plan. 2. Intuitivly knowing how to flow through the many combined tools within this app. 3. Seeing where and how to do what they want. 4. No confusion on how to do the initial setup and flow to building/enabling profiles and building/modifying their cooresponding description templates.  5. web only, I create a pwa app, I also created a dead simple andoid widget apk so I can have a homescreen widget that shows todays scheduled run distance. That is the extent of mobile needs and this is already implemented. 6. Touch: Dashboard, Plan, Control. Mouse/Keyboard: Build, Sources. 7. no. 8. Just docker and github. 9 validation. api connections, description, profile/workout/template creation are valid and sound. 10. Installing, initial configuring, customizing, understanding jinja. 11. Pushing to garmin. 12. When they check strava after a run and laugh at a funny whimsical metric about their run that they configured. 13. Getting all the sources loaded and successfully connected. 14. Not able to set up strava My API App, not understanding what profiles are and not understanding how to build a template from scratch.

1. Wow, this looks so modern and premium. I feel like I am in control of everything 2. Check out this really cool automated description! 3. Excited to go on the next run. 4. Garmin: 'here is your data', Strava: 'heres some data, heres some influencers, heres some clubs/badges/coments/kudos/...' Chronicle: 'Here's your story'. 5. Fun, Creative, Motivating 6. They are being led through the process, guided, shown that it's not so hard. 7. Like they can experiment/add/subtract/reorder, while seeing if their plan has any risk factors visually. 8. Big Grin! Locked into analyzing the description, noting noteables and keeping a mental model of run-over-run trends and comparing normally hidden values with others (vo2max, hill score, etc etc). 9. Like all they need to do is restart the docker.

Strava, Garmin, SmashRun, Intervals.icu, custom github projects like Statistics-for-strava, garmin_planner, git-sweaty, Strautomator, activity-craft. What I want is not to remake those apps but have a UX that integrates everything seamlessly together so it doesn't look like a frankenstein of disjointed components.

I like 2. Established system, speed and maintainability matter more, I already created a logo icon and banner, I cannot program and have never created any piece of software this all comes down to enabling the ai agent to produce the best UI

1. "check out my custom description!" 2. Ease of set up and lower time to working output. 3. no clue, probably through custom python scripts or paid services. 4. Bring my data locally - from there do what I want with it. 5. Low touch. 6. Descriptions are reliably set and just there. 7. It should feel fast, very snappy, no reason to be as bloaty as the big guys. 8. This is novel that I have not seen anywhere else.
I was using a "neon orange strava darkmode black with dark gray ghosting" I want this to feel premium, luxury, porsche
1. I like more accents, but I will leave the color desin and the pops of color up to the designer. 2. sleek modern. 3. balanced. 4. follow standard AA

Under AI Agent Guidelines, please ensure that for every feature or phase use and documentation via github is strictly enforced, I want the agents to follow the following GITHUB as Memory protocol: ## GitHub-as-Memory Workflows (Agentic)

This repo uses GitHub Issues + PRs as durable "project memory" so work can resume after context compaction without rehydrating the entire history.
You are in a sandbox, I want you to use gh, git, and connect to the docker hub but you must ask for permission to use those commands, I will approve but if you do not ask those commands can fail and that can interupt workflow.

### Principles (Token-Aware)
- Do NOT paste long logs, stack traces, or CI output into chat context unless directly needed.
- Store bulk artifacts in GitHub instead:
  - Issue body = canonical problem statement + acceptance criteria + current status
  - PR description = canonical solution narrative + test plan + verification
  - CI logs stay in Actions; summarize the failure in one paragraph and link via PR/Actions
- When resuming, use the Recall Protocol to pull only the minimum relevant state.

---

## Labels & Conventions
Use labels consistently so recall/search is fast.

**Type**
- `type/bug`, `type/feat`, `type/docs`, `type/refactor`, `type/chore`

**Priority**
- `prio/high`, `prio/med`, `prio/low`

**State**
- `state/blocked`, `state/needs-info`, `state/in-progress`

**Agent**
- `agent/ready`, `agent/wip`, `agent/do-not-touch`

**Branch naming**
- With an issue: `type/<ISSUE>-short-kebab`
  - `fix/123-dashboard-leak`
  - `feat/221-add-auth`
- Without an issue (tiny changes only): `type/short-kebab`

**PR title / squash commit title**
- Conventional commit style: `type(scope): description`
- Example: `fix(api): handle null profile response`

---

## Recall Protocol (Use BEFORE starting work or after compaction)
When the user says "resume", "continue", "pick up", or references a bug/feature without details:

1. Identify the active work item:
   - If an issue number is known: use it.
   - Else search GitHub by keywords.

2. Minimal GitHub recall (prefer CLI):
   - Find candidates:
     - `gh issue list --state open --limit 20`
     - `gh issue list --search "<keywords>" --state open --limit 20`
     - `gh pr list --state open --limit 20`
   - Open the best match:
     - `gh issue view <N> --comments`
     - `gh pr view <N> --comments`
     - `gh pr checks <N>`

3. Extract ONLY these into working context (5–10 bullets max):
   - Problem statement (Observed vs Expected)
   - Acceptance criteria (checkboxes)
   - Current status (what’s done / what’s blocked)
   - Repro steps (or how to validate)
   - Most recent CI failure summary (if any)
   - Next 1–3 actions

4. If anything is missing (repro steps, expected behavior, env), label `state/needs-info` and ask ONLY for that missing detail.

---

## Workflow A: "New Issue" (GitHub Memory First)
When the user says: "new issue", "track this", "file this", or a bug feels non-trivial (>30 min, unclear, likely to recur):

### A1) Decide: Issue vs No Issue
Create an issue if ANY are true:
- Bug requires reproduction steps or logs to preserve
- Multi-step fix, refactor, or risk of regression
- Likely to be revisited later
- Requires discussion/decision

Skip issue only for tiny one-commit changes (typo, trivial docs).

### A2) Create Issue (CLI preferred)
1. Create issue with structured body:
   - `gh issue create`
2. Add labels:
   - `type/*`, `prio/*`, optional `state/needs-info` if unclear
3. Issue body MUST include:
   - **Observed**
   - **Expected**
   - **Repro steps**
   - **Scope / constraints**
   - **Acceptance criteria** (checkbox list)
   - **Current status** (short; update as you progress)

### A3) Add a Plan Comment (keeps memory durable)
Add a brief comment with:
- Proposed approach (3–7 steps)
- Risks/unknowns
- Test plan (what will prove it works)

### A4) Create Branch from Issue
1. `git checkout main`
2. `git pull --ff-only`
3. `git checkout -b <type>/<ISSUE>-short-kebab`

### A5) Work Loop (keep commits meaningful)
- Keep commits small and descriptive.
- Reference issue in PR (not necessarily every commit).
- If you discover new info (root cause, constraints), UPDATE the issue body under **Current status**.

### A6) Open PR Early (Draft by default)
1. Push branch:
   - `git push -u origin HEAD`
2. Create PR (draft preferred while iterating):
   - `gh pr create --draft --fill`
3. In PR description, include:
   - Summary (what/why)
   - Test plan (what you ran locally)
   - Verification steps (how to validate manually)
   - Risks / rollback notes (if relevant)
   - Link issue: include `Fixes #<ISSUE>` ONLY when confident it should auto-close on merge

### A7) CI loop
- Watch checks:
  - `gh pr checks --watch`
- If CI fails:
  - Summarize failure in PR comment (1 short paragraph + next action)
  - Fix and push until green

---

## Workflow B: "Commit / Push / CI" (your existing habit, formalized)
When the user says "commit/push/ci" (or you’re ready to checkpoint work):

1. Ensure branch is correct and named per convention.
2. Run local quality gate (single command if possible, e.g. `make check`):
   - lint/format, unit tests, typecheck, build (as applicable)
3. Commit with conventional commit format:
   - `type(scope): description`
   - Body for non-trivial changes: context + tradeoffs
4. Push:
   - `git push`
5. Ensure a PR exists (create/update as needed):
   - `gh pr create --draft --fill` (if none exists)
6. Watch CI:
   - `gh pr checks --watch`
7. Update issue **Current status** (only if there is an issue):
   - What changed
   - CI status
   - Next action

---

## Workflow C: "Merge to Main" (Clean Main, CI-Enforced)
When the user says "merge to main":

### C1) Preconditions
- A PR exists targeting `main`
- CI is green (required checks)
- Issue linkage is correct (`Fixes #N` if applicable)
- No unresolved review/notes (even if solo: read your own PR once)

### C2) Sync & Conflict Check (branch stays current)
1. `git fetch origin`
2. Ensure PR branch is up-to-date with `origin/main` (repo standard):
   - Preferred: rebase
     - `git rebase origin/main`
   - If conflicts:
     - resolve, rerun checks, push

### C3) Final verification (minimal but real)
- Confirm local checks passed OR rely on CI + a smoke check script.
- PR description includes verification steps.
- If your app is Docker-based, ensure:
  - image build is successful in CI
  - version/tag semantics are correct (if you tag)

### C4) Merge method
Default: **Squash merge** (keeps main readable; PR becomes the story).
- Squash title MUST remain conventional-commit formatted.
- If PR contains multiple logical changes, prefer multiple PRs rather than merge commits.

CLI merge:
- `gh pr merge <PR#> --squash --delete-branch`

### C5) Post-merge hygiene
1. Update local main:
   - `git checkout main`
   - `git pull --ff-only`
2. Confirm issue auto-closed (if `Fixes #N` used).
3. Update the issue (if still open) with:
   - What shipped
   - How to validate
   - Any follow-ups (new issues if needed)

---

## What to store where (memory map)
- **Issue**: problem, repro, acceptance criteria, current status, key decisions
- **PR**: solution narrative, test plan, verification, risk/rollback
- **docs/adr/** (optional): architectural decisions that outlive the issue
- **Actions logs**: keep there; summarize outcomes in PR comments
